---
title: "Drivers of Lasting Streaming Performance"
subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project"
author: "Mike Stanton"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/watercolour_sys02_img34_teacup-ocean.jpg
  data-background-size: stretch
  data-background-opacity: "0.7"
  data-slide-number: none
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:  ['data/customtheming.scss']
  
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
#| include: false

# Load packages here
pacman::p_load(tidymodels,
               tidyverse,
               plotly,
               lubridate)

```

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Plot theme
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11))

# For better figure resolution
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  fig.retina = 3, 
  dpi = 300, 
  fig.width = 6, 
  fig.asp = 0.618 
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: load-data
#| include: false
# Load data here
movies <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-07-29/movies.csv')
shows <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-07-29/shows.csv')

all_data_raw <- bind_rows(movies, shows)
print(all_data_raw)
```

```{r}

# 1. Clean and Transform
all_data_cleaned <- all_data_raw %>%
  # Ensure release_date is a Date object and drop missing values
  filter(!is.na(release_date), !is.na(hours_viewed)) %>%
  
  # Map your report_period IDs to an actual anchor Date for age calculations
  # Adjust these dates based on the exact coverage of the 6-month reports
  mutate(
    report_date = case_when(
      report == "2023Jul-Dec" ~ as.Date("2023-12-31"),
      report == "2024Jan-Jun" ~ as.Date("2024-06-30"),
      report == "2024Jul-Dec" ~ as.Date("2024-12-31"),
      report == "2025Jan-Jun" ~ as.Date("2025-06-30"),
      TRUE ~ as.Date("2025-06-30")
    )
  ) %>%
  
  # CRITICAL FIX: Calculate age relative to release (all titles now start at 0)
  mutate(
    age_in_weeks = as.numeric(difftime(report_date, release_date, units = "weeks"))
  ) %>%
  
  # Filter out negative ages and truncate extreme long-tails for comparison readability
  filter(age_in_weeks >= 0) %>% # Looking at the first 3 years of life max
  
  # Round to weeks for grouping calculations
  mutate(age_binned = round(age_in_weeks)) %>%
  
# Extract the exact calendar year from the release date
  mutate(
    release_year_num = lubridate::year(release_date)
  ) %>%
  
  # Optional but recommended: Filter out very old sparse data 
  # so you only look at the most robust years (e.g., 2015 to 2025)
  filter(release_year_num >= 2015) %>% 
  
  # Convert to a factor/categorical variable for ggplot colors
  mutate(
    release_year = as.factor(release_year_num)
  )

# 2. Aggregate Data
aggregated_trends <- all_data_cleaned %>%
  group_by(release_year, age_binned) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_hours = mean(hours_viewed, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_views = if("views" %in% names(.)) mean(views, na.rm = TRUE) else mean(hours_viewed / 2, na.rm = TRUE), 
    title_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>% # Keep the pipe operator here
  
  # ADD THIS NEW MUTATE FUNCTION HERE:
  mutate(
    log_views = log10(avg_views),
    log_hours = log10(avg_hours)
  )



```






```{r}

#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 7
#| out-width: "100%"

library(crosstalk)
library(htmltools)

# 1. Build Plot A: Total Views (Perfect and Untouched)
p1 <- ggplot(aggregated_trends, aes(x = age_binned, y = log_views, color = release_year, group = release_year)) +
  geom_smooth(aes(
    alpha = title_count,
    text = paste0("Year: ", release_year, "<br>Age: ", age_binned, " weeks<br>Avg Views: ", scales::comma(avg_views), "<br>Titles: ", title_count)
  ), method = "loess", se = FALSE, span = 0.4, size = 1.2) +
  scale_alpha_continuous(range = c(0.4, 1.0), guide = "none") + 
  labs(
    title = "Content Trends by Release Year: Total Views",
    x = "Weeks Since Release (Aligned to Week 0)",
    y = "Log10 of Average Views",
    color = "Release Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(~ release_year, scales = "free_x", ncol = 4) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "none", 
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10)
  )

# 2. Build Plot B: Hours Watched (Perfect and Untouched)
p2 <- ggplot(aggregated_trends, aes(x = age_binned, y = log_hours, color = release_year, group = release_year)) +
  geom_smooth(aes(
    alpha = title_count,
    text = paste0("Year: ", release_year, "<br>Age: ", age_binned, " weeks<br>Avg Hours: ", scales::comma(avg_hours), "<br>Titles: ", title_count)
  ), method = "loess", se = FALSE, span = 0.4, size = 1.2) +
  scale_alpha_continuous(range = c(0.4, 1.0), guide = "none") + 
  labs(
    title = "Content Trends by Release Year: Hours Watched",
    x = "Weeks Since Release (Aligned to Week 0)",
    y = "Log10 of Average Hours Watched", 
    color = "Release Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(~ release_year, scales = "free_x", ncol = 4) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "none", 
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10)
  )

# Convert both cleanly to independent, crisp plotly objects
plotly_views <- ggplotly(p1, width = 1150, height = 550, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(margin = list(t = 80, b = 60, l = 70, r = 40))

plotly_hours <- ggplotly(p2, width = 1150, height = 550, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(margin = list(t = 80, b = 60, l = 70, r = 40))

# 3. Create a pristine HTML/JavaScript container with native toggle buttons
# This bypasses the buggy internal plotly layout engine completely
dashboard_html <- tags$div(
  tags$div(
    style = "margin-bottom: 15px;",
    tags$button("View by Total Views", onclick = "document.getElementById('views-plot').style.display='block'; document.getElementById('hours-plot').style.display='none'; this.className='btn btn-primary'; this.nextElementSibling.className='btn btn-outline-primary';", class="btn btn-primary", style="margin-right: 10px;"),
    tags$button("View by Hours Watched", onclick = "document.getElementById('views-plot').style.display='none'; document.getElementById('hours-plot').style.display='block'; this.previousElementSibling.className='btn btn-outline-primary'; this.className='btn btn-primary';", class="btn btn-outline-primary")
  ),
  tags$div(id = "views-plot", style = "display: block;", plotly_views),
  tags$div(id = "hours-plot", style = "display: none;", plotly_hours)
)

# Render the clean dashboard structure
browsable(dashboard_html)


```

## QUESTION 2



```{r clean-data}
movies_data <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-07-29/movies.csv')
shows_data <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-07-29/shows.csv')

# 1. Defensive function to convert "1H 54M", "2H", or "45M" to total minutes
parse_runtime <- function(runtime_str) {
  hours <- as.numeric(str_match(runtime_str, "(\\d+)H")[, 2])
  minutes <- as.numeric(str_match(runtime_str, "(\\d+)M")[, 2])
  
  hours <- ifelse(is.na(hours), 0, hours)
  minutes <- ifelse(is.na(minutes), 0, minutes)
  
  return((hours * 60) + minutes)
}

# 2. Clean Movies
movies_clean <- movies_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(runtime)) %>%
  mutate(
    type = "Movie",
    runtime_minutes = parse_runtime(runtime),
    # Use views if it exists, otherwise calculate it
    views_calculated = if("views" %in% names(.)) as.numeric(views) else as.numeric(hours_viewed) / (runtime_minutes / 60)
  )

# 3. Clean Shows
shows_clean <- shows_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(runtime)) %>%
  mutate(
    type = "Show",
    runtime_minutes = parse_runtime(runtime),
    views_calculated = if("views" %in% names(.)) as.numeric(views) else as.numeric(hours_viewed) / (runtime_minutes / 60)
  )

# 4. Standardized Combine
combined_base <- bind_rows(movies_clean, shows_clean)
```

```{r chunk-2-dates}
combined_dates <- combined_base %>%
  mutate(
    # Safely convert release_date to an R Date object
    release_date_clean = as.Date(release_date),
    
    # Use lubridate's ymd() or parse_date_time() to extract the date from strings like "2025Jan..."
    # This automatically looks for a 4-digit year, month name/abbreviation, and day if present
    report_date_clean = parse_date_time(report, orders = c("Y", "Ym", "Ybd", "ymd")),
    report_date_clean = as.Date(report_date_clean), # Ensure it's a strict Date class
    
    # Calculate exact weeks elapsed
    weeks_since_release = floor(as.numeric(report_date_clean - release_date_clean) / 7)
  )



```

```{r}

# 2. Add buckets without aggressive filtering yet
combined_bucketed <- combined_dates %>%
  mutate(
    duration_bucket = case_when(
      type == "Movie" & runtime_minutes < 90 ~ "Short (<90 mins)",
      type == "Movie" & runtime_minutes >= 90 & runtime_minutes <= 130 ~ "Standard (90-130 mins)",
      type == "Movie" & runtime_minutes > 130 ~ "Epic (>130 mins)",
      
      type == "Show" & runtime_minutes < 240 ~ "Short/Mini-Series (<4 hrs)",
      type == "Show" & runtime_minutes >= 240 & runtime_minutes <= 480 ~ "Standard Season (4-8 hrs)",
      type == "Show" & runtime_minutes > 480 ~ "Binge/Long Season (>8 hrs)",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

# 3. Aggregate safely (we will filter downstream instead of upstream)
weekly_trends <- combined_bucketed %>%
  group_by(type, duration_bucket, weeks_since_release) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_views = mean(views, na.rm = TRUE),
    title_count = n_distinct(title),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  # Filter only positive weeks, up to 24 weeks
  filter(!is.na(weeks_since_release), weeks_since_release >= 0, weeks_since_release <= 24)

```


```{r native-plotly-toggle, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 7
#| out-width: "100%"

library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

# Split the data into separate dataframes for pristine control
movie_trends <- weekly_trends %>% filter(type == "Movie")
show_trends  <- weekly_trends %>% filter(type == "Show")

# Get unique buckets to loop through/map consistently
movie_buckets <- unique(movie_trends$duration_bucket)
show_buckets  <- unique(show_trends$duration_bucket)

# Initialize the plot
p <- plot_ly()

# 1. Add MOVIE lines (Traces 1 to N)
for(bucket in movie_buckets) {
  bucket_data <- movie_trends %>% filter(duration_bucket == bucket)
  p <- p %>% add_trace(
    data = bucket_data,
    x = ~weeks_since_release,
    y = ~avg_views,
    name = bucket,
    type = 'scatter',
    mode = 'lines', # or 'lines+markers'
    visible = TRUE, # Movies visible by default
    hovertemplate = paste0("<b>", bucket, "</b><br>Week: %{x}<br>Avg Views: %{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
  )
}

# 2. Add SHOW lines (Traces N+1 to M)
for(bucket in show_buckets) {
  bucket_data <- show_trends %>% filter(duration_bucket == bucket)
  p <- p %>% add_trace(
    data = bucket_data,
    x = ~weeks_since_release,
    y = ~avg_views,
    name = bucket,
    type = 'scatter',
    mode = 'lines',
    visible = FALSE, # Hidden by default
    hovertemplate = paste0("<b>", bucket, "</b><br>Week: %{x}<br>Avg Views: %{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
  )
}

# 3. Define the visibility vectors explicitly
# We need a TRUE/FALSE flag for every single trace we just created
total_movie_traces <- length(movie_buckets)
total_show_traces  <- length(show_buckets)

movie_visibility <- c(rep(TRUE, total_movie_traces), rep(FALSE, total_show_traces))
show_visibility  <- c(rep(FALSE, total_movie_traces), rep(TRUE, total_show_traces))

# 4. Add the layout and the working toggle buttons
p %>% layout(
  title = list(text = "Content Decay Curves: Lasting Popularity by Runtime", y = 0.95),
  
  # IMPROVEMENT: Clean up the axes and add vertical tracking lines on hover
  xaxis = list(
    title = "Weeks Since Release", 
    dtick = 4,
    showspikes = TRUE,          # Turns on vertical hover guide line
    spikemode = "across",
    spikesnap = "cursor",
    spikethickness = 1,
    spikedash = "dash"
  ),
  yaxis = list(
    title = "Average Views",
    tickvals = seq(0, 25000000, by = 5000000),
    ticktext = c("0 ", "5 Million ", "10 Million ", "15 Million ", "20 Million ", "25 Million "),
    tickmargin = 20     # Adds extra padding so the long text doesn't crowd the axis line
  ),
  
  # IMPROVEMENT: Lift the buttons slightly higher (from 1.12 to 1.16)
  margin = list(t = 100),
  hovermode = "x unified",
  updatemenus = list(
    list(
      type = "buttons",
      direction = "right",
      x = 0.5,
      y = 1.16, 
      xanchor = "center",
      active = 0,
      buttons = list(
        list(
          method = "restyle",
          args = list("visible", as.list(movie_visibility)),
          label = "View Movies"
        ),
        list(
          method = "restyle",
          args = list("visible", as.list(show_visibility)),
          label = "View Shows"
        )
      )
    )
  )
)
```












# Using Quarto for presentations

## Quarto

- The presentation is created using the Quarto CLI

- `##` sets the start of a new slide

## Layouts

You can use plain text

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
- or bullet points[^1]
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
or in two columns
:::
:::::

[^1]: And add footnotes

- like

- this

## Code

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#model <- lm(mpg ~ speed, data = mtcars) 

#model |> tidy()

#model |> glance()

```

## Plots

```{r}
penguins |>
  mutate(species = ifelse(species == "Adelie", "Adelie", "Other")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point()
```

## Plot and text

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Some text

- goes here
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, warning=FALSE, fig.width=5.5}
penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = species, color = species)) +
  geom_boxplot(linewidth = 0.75,
               outlier.size = 2.5) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15) +
  theme(legend.key.size = unit(0.8, "cm"))
```
:::
:::::

# A new section...

## Tables

If you want to generate a table, make sure it is in the HTML format (instead of Markdown or other formats), e.g.,

```{r}
#penguins |> 
  #head() |>
  #kableExtra::kable() |>
  #kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

## Images

![Image credit: Danielle Navarro, Percolate.](images/watercolour_sys02_img34_teacup-ocean.png){fig-align="center" width="500"}

## Math Expressions {.smaller}

You can write LaTeX math expressions inside a pair of dollar signs, e.g. \$\\alpha+\\beta\$ renders $\alpha + \beta$. You can use the display style with double dollar signs:

```         
$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$
```

$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$

Limitations:

1.  The source code of a LaTeX math expression must be in one line, unless it is inside a pair of double dollar signs, in which case the starting `$$` must appear in the very beginning of a line, followed immediately by a non-space character, and the ending `$$` must be at the end of a line, led by a non-space character;

2.  There should not be spaces after the opening `$` or before the closing `$`.

# Wrap up

## Feeling adventurous?

- You are welcomed to use the default styling of the slides. In fact, that's what I expect majority of you will do. You will differentiate yourself with the content of your presentation.

- But some of you might want to play around with slide styling. Some solutions for this can be found at https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs.
